meaning_en: something very easy to do
example_en: The exam was a piece of cake — I finished in twenty minutes.

meaning_en: good luck (especially before a performance)
example_en: Break a leg tonight — you've rehearsed this play for months!

meaning_en: to study hard
example_en: I can't go out tonight — I have to hit the books before the final.

idiom: Let the cat out of the bag
meaning_en: to reveal a secret, often by accident
example_en: She let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.

meaning_en: feeling slightly ill
example_en: I'm a bit under the weather today — I think I'll stay home.

meaning_en: to do something unpleasant that you have been avoiding
example_en: I hate going to the dentist, but I'll have to bite the bullet.

meaning_en: to do or say something to make people feel relaxed
example_en: He told a joke to break the ice at the meeting.

idiom: Hit the nail on the head
meaning_en: to describe exactly what is causing a situation or problem
example_en: You hit the nail on the head when you said the project lacked focus.

translation_ru: крайне редко, раз в сто лет
meaning_en: very rarely
example_en: We only see each other once in a blue moon these days.

meaning_en: to reveal secret information
example_en: Come on, spill the beans — what did he tell you?

idiom: The ball is in your court
meaning_en: it is your responsibility to take the next action
example_en: I've made my offer, so the ball is in your court now.

idiom: Cost an arm and a leg
meaning_en: to be very expensive
example_en: That new car cost him an arm and a leg.

meaning_en: to do something in the easiest or cheapest way, often badly
example_en: The builders cut corners and now the roof leaks.

meaning_en: to become difficult to control
example_en: The party got out of hand around midnight.

meaning_en: to go to bed
example_en: I'm exhausted — I'm going to hit the sack.

meaning_en: it is not difficult to understand
example_en: Come on, making coffee isn't rocket science.

meaning_en: to tell someone something untrue as a joke
example_en: Don't worry, I was just pulling your leg — there's no test today.

meaning_en: things are going well up to now
example_en: The renovation? So far so good — we're on schedule.

meaning_en: said when the person you were talking about appears
example_en: Speak of the devil — here comes Mark now!

meaning_en: the final problem in a series that makes you lose patience
example_en: He was late again — that's the last straw, he's fired.

meaning_en: to avoid talking about what is important
example_en: Stop beating around the bush and tell me what happened.

meaning_en: it is better for someone to arrive or do something late than not at all
example_en: He apologised after a year, but better late than never.

idiom: Bite off more than you can chew
meaning_en: to try to do something that is too difficult for you
example_en: I bit off more than I could chew when I agreed to redesign the whole site alone.

idiom: By the skin of your teeth
meaning_en: only just; barely
example_en: We caught the train by the skin of our teeth.

meaning_en: to stop working on something
example_en: We've been at this for hours — let's call it a day.

translation_ru: плакать о том, чего не вернёшь
meaning_en: to be upset about something that has already happened
example_en: Yes, you made a mistake, but there's no point crying over spilled milk.

meaning_en: being too inquisitive can lead to trouble
example_en: Don't ask too many questions — curiosity killed the cat.

idiom: Don't judge a book by its cover
meaning_en: don't form an opinion based only on appearance
example_en: He looks rough, but he's the kindest man I know — don't judge a book by its cover.

idiom: Every cloud has a silver lining
meaning_en: there is something positive in every difficult situation
example_en: Losing that job was hard, but every cloud has a silver lining — I found a better one.

idiom: Get a taste of your own medicine
meaning_en: to be treated the same unpleasant way you treat others
example_en: He's always rude to waiters; today one was rude back — a taste of his own medicine.

meaning_en: to deliberately ignore someone
example_en: Ever since our argument, she's been giving me the cold shoulder.

meaning_en: to make more effort than is expected of you
example_en: Our team always goes the extra mile for our customers.

meaning_en: don't give up
example_en: I know exams are tough — hang in there, you're almost done.

meaning_en: to leave or begin a journey
example_en: It's getting late — we should hit the road.

meaning_en: in trouble
example_en: He's in hot water with his boss for missing the deadline.

meaning_en: both people involved in a situation are responsible for it
example_en: Don't blame only her — it takes two to tango.

meaning_en: to join an activity that has become popular
example_en: Once the diet became trendy, everyone jumped on the bandwagon.

meaning_en: stay cheerful in a difficult situation
example_en: Keep your chin up — things will get better soon.

idiom: Kill two birds with one stone
meaning_en: to achieve two things with a single action
example_en: By cycling to work I kill two birds with one stone — exercise and commute.

translation_ru: перевернуть всё вверх дном в поисках
meaning_en: to do everything possible to find something
example_en: Detectives left no stone unturned in the investigation.

meaning_en: to omit details and get to the point
example_en: To make a long story short, we got lost and missed the flight.

meaning_en: to miss an opportunity
example_en: If you don't apply today, you'll miss the boat.

translation_ru: без труда не выловишь и рыбку
meaning_en: you have to suffer to achieve something good
example_en: Training hurts, but no pain, no gain.

meaning_en: extremely happy
example_en: She's been on cloud nine since she got engaged.

meaning_en: alert and quick to understand
example_en: Our new assistant is really on the ball.

meaning_en: completely unexpectedly
example_en: He called me out of the blue after ten years.

meaning_en: to control your emotions and behave calmly
example_en: Pull yourself together — crying won't help now.

meaning_en: to spoil someone's plans or pleasure
example_en: I hate to rain on your parade, but the picnic is cancelled.

meaning_en: to agree with someone
example_en: My sister and I rarely see eye to eye on politics.

meaning_en: to avoid making a decision or choice
example_en: Stop sitting on the fence — are you with us or not?

meaning_en: to take attention or praise away from someone
example_en: She stole my thunder by announcing her promotion at my birthday dinner.

idiom: Take it with a grain of salt
meaning_en: to not completely believe something
example_en: He exaggerates a lot, so take his stories with a grain of salt.

meaning_en: the advantages of two different things at once
example_en: Working from home gives me the best of both worlds — flexibility and focus.

meaning_en: to admit defeat and stop trying
example_en: After three failed attempts, he threw in the towel.

meaning_en: time passes very quickly
example_en: Time flies when you're having fun.

meaning_en: to make a bad situation even worse
example_en: We were lost, and to make matters worse, it started raining.

meaning_en: to understand something difficult
example_en: I can't wrap my head around how she finished so fast.

idiom: You can't have your cake and eat it too
meaning_en: you can't have two desirable but incompatible things
example_en: You want a high salary and short hours? You can't have your cake and eat it too.

translation_ru: не было бы счастья, да несчастье помогло
meaning_en: something that seems bad but turns out to be good
example_en: Missing that flight was a blessing in disguise — the next one had an empty row.

meaning_en: very common and of no special value
example_en: Cafés like this are a dime a dozen in the city centre.

meaning_en: what people do is more important than what they say
example_en: He promised to help, but actions speak louder than words.

meaning_en: to make a bad situation worse
example_en: He forgot her birthday, and to add insult to injury, he went out with friends.

meaning_en: rushed and short of time
example_en: We're working against the clock to ship the update tonight.

meaning_en: fully listening
example_en: Tell me about your trip — I'm all ears.

meaning_en: in the same difficult situation
example_en: Don't worry, we're all in the same boat with these new rules.

meaning_en: a person who is loved more than any other
example_en: His youngest daughter is the apple of his eye.

meaning_en: very easy
example_en: Setting up the printer was as easy as pie.

idiom: At the drop of a hat
meaning_en: immediately, without hesitation
example_en: She'd travel anywhere at the drop of a hat.

meaning_en: back to the starting point with no progress
example_en: The deal fell through, so we're back to square one.

meaning_en: to start planning something again because the first plan failed
example_en: The prototype didn't work — back to the drawing board.

meaning_en: to be mistaken about the cause or solution
example_en: If you think I broke it, you're barking up the wrong tree.

meaning_en: to waste effort on something that has no chance of succeeding
example_en: Arguing about it now is beating a dead horse.

meaning_en: to try very hard to please someone
example_en: She bent over backwards to make her guests comfortable.

meaning_en: an excellent new invention or idea
example_en: He thinks his new app is the best thing since sliced bread.

idiom: Birds of a feather flock together
meaning_en: people of similar character tend to associate
example_en: Those two are always together — birds of a feather flock together.

meaning_en: family relationships are stronger than other ties
example_en: He chose to help his brother — blood is thicker than water.

meaning_en: to destroy relationships permanently
example_en: Don't burn your bridges with your old employer.

meaning_en: to work late into the night
example_en: I burned the midnight oil to finish the report.

meaning_en: to make peace after an argument
example_en: After years of feuding, the brothers finally buried the hatchet.

meaning_en: unable to choose between two alternatives
example_en: I'm caught between two stools — both job offers are great.

meaning_en: caught in the act of doing something wrong
example_en: The thief was caught red-handed leaving the shop.

meaning_en: a long-held resentment or grievance
example_en: He has a chip on his shoulder about not going to university.

idiom: Cross that bridge when you come to it
meaning_en: to deal with a problem only when it arises
example_en: We don't know if it'll rain — we'll cross that bridge when we come to it.

meaning_en: to get to the point without wasting time
example_en: Cut to the chase — what do you actually want?

meaning_en: someone who argues an opposing view for the sake of debate
example_en: Let me play devil's advocate for a moment.

meaning_en: until the very last possible moment
example_en: The election went down to the wire.

meaning_en: a very small amount compared to what is needed
example_en: The donation is generous, but it's a drop in the ocean.

meaning_en: more difficult to do than to talk about
example_en: Quitting sugar is easier said than done.

meaning_en: to admit you were wrong
example_en: He had to eat humble pie when his prediction failed.

meaning_en: an obvious problem no one wants to discuss
example_en: Nobody mentioned the budget cuts — the elephant in the room.

meaning_en: to accept the unpleasant consequences of your actions
example_en: Sooner or later he'll have to face the music.

meaning_en: uncomfortable in an unfamiliar situation
example_en: I felt like a fish out of water at the formal dinner.

meaning_en: in very good health
example_en: My grandfather is ninety and fit as a fiddle.

meaning_en: to suddenly become nervous about doing something
example_en: He got cold feet the night before the wedding.

meaning_en: to organise yourself and behave responsibly
example_en: You need to get your act together before the deadline.

meaning_en: to attempt something
example_en: I've never skied before, but I'll give it a shot.

meaning_en: to fail spectacularly
example_en: The startup went down in flames within a year.

meaning_en: obvious; not needing to be stated
example_en: It goes without saying that we're grateful.

meaning_en: unable to act because of rules or circumstances
example_en: I'd love to help, but my hands are tied.

meaning_en: to enjoy yourself a lot
example_en: We had a blast at the concert last night.

meaning_en: to have more important things to do
example_en: I can't deal with this now — I have bigger fish to fry.

meaning_en: completely in love
example_en: He's head over heels for his new girlfriend.

meaning_en: to reach the lowest possible point
example_en: After losing everything, he hit rock bottom.

meaning_en: wait a moment; slow down
example_en: Hold your horses — let me finish my sentence.

meaning_en: very briefly
example_en: In a nutshell, the project was a success.

idiom: In the heat of the moment
meaning_en: while too excited or angry to think clearly
example_en: I said things in the heat of the moment that I now regret.

meaning_en: said when you meet someone you know unexpectedly
example_en: You know my cousin? It's a small world!

meaning_en: to do something too soon
example_en: Don't jump the gun — wait for the official announcement.

meaning_en: to watch carefully
example_en: Could you keep an eye on my bag for a moment?

meaning_en: to hope something will happen the way you want
example_en: Keep your fingers crossed for my interview tomorrow.

meaning_en: to die (informal, often humorous)
example_en: The old fridge finally kicked the bucket last week.

meaning_en: to understand how something works
example_en: Ask Maria — she knows the ropes around here.

translation_ru: и наконец, что не менее важно
meaning_en: the final item, but no less important than the others
example_en: And last but not least, I want to thank my family.

meaning_en: to help someone
example_en: Could you lend a hand with these boxes?

translation_ru: не буди лихо, пока спит тихо
meaning_en: to leave a situation alone to avoid causing trouble
example_en: Don't bring up the argument — let sleeping dogs lie.

idiom: Light at the end of the tunnel
meaning_en: a sign that a difficult time is ending
example_en: After months of work, I finally see light at the end of the tunnel.

meaning_en: to have just enough money for what you need
example_en: With three kids, it's hard to make ends meet.

meaning_en: to fail to achieve the intended result
example_en: His joke missed the mark — nobody laughed.

meaning_en: money is not easy to get
example_en: You can't buy that — money doesn't grow on trees.

meaning_en: to stop something at an early stage
example_en: We need to nip this rumour in the bud.

meaning_en: with no special conditions or restrictions
example_en: It's a free trial, no strings attached.

meaning_en: no longer in trouble
example_en: She told the truth, so I'm off the hook.

idiom: Off the top of my head
meaning_en: without taking time to think carefully
example_en: Off the top of my head, I'd say about fifty people came.

translation_ru: на тонком льду, в опасном положении
meaning_en: in a risky situation
example_en: After missing two deadlines, he's on thin ice.

idiom: Out of the frying pan into the fire
meaning_en: from a bad situation to a worse one
example_en: Switching jobs was out of the frying pan into the fire.

meaning_en: extremely pleased
example_en: She was over the moon about her promotion.

meaning_en: an annoying person or thing
example_en: Filing taxes is a real pain in the neck.

meaning_en: what are you thinking about?
example_en: You've been quiet all evening — penny for your thoughts?

meaning_en: to do extra work that someone else has not done
example_en: When she went on leave, we all picked up the slack.

meaning_en: to decide what to do as the situation develops
example_en: We don't have firm plans — we'll play it by ear.

meaning_en: doing something repeatedly improves your skill
example_en: Don't be discouraged — practice makes perfect.

idiom: Put all your eggs in one basket
translation_ru: складывать все яйца в одну корзину
meaning_en: to risk everything on a single venture
example_en: Diversify your investments — don't put all your eggs in one basket.

idiom: Put your foot in your mouth
meaning_en: to say something embarrassing or tactless
example_en: I really put my foot in my mouth when I asked about her ex.

meaning_en: to understand a hidden meaning
example_en: Read between the lines — he's clearly unhappy with the offer.

meaning_en: perfectly fine, especially in health
example_en: After a good sleep, I'll be right as rain.

idiom: Rome wasn't built in a day
meaning_en: important things take time
example_en: Be patient with the renovation — Rome wasn't built in a day.

meaning_en: to annoy or irritate someone
example_en: His attitude rubs me the wrong way.

meaning_en: rescued from a difficult situation just in time
example_en: The phone rang during the awkward silence — saved by the bell.

meaning_en: very annoyed by something
example_en: I'm sick and tired of these excuses.

meaning_en: to delay a decision until the next day
example_en: It's a big decision — sleep on it.

meaning_en: to suspect something is wrong
example_en: When he changed the subject, I smelled a rat.

meaning_en: to refuse to change your opinion or plan
example_en: Stick to your guns — don't let them pressure you.

meaning_en: to decline an offer with the suggestion of accepting later
example_en: I can't make dinner tonight — can I take a rain check?

meaning_en: to relax or not work hard
example_en: Take it easy — there's no rush.

meaning_en: to be very anxious or frustrated
example_en: I was tearing my hair out trying to fix the bug.

meaning_en: the most important parts of a situation
example_en: Try to look at the bigger picture, not just today's setback.

meaning_en: a harsh but real fact
example_en: The cold hard truth is that we can't afford it.

idiom: The early bird catches the worm
translation_ru: кто рано встаёт, тому Бог подаёт
meaning_en: those who arrive first have the best chance of success
example_en: Get there early — the early bird catches the worm.

meaning_en: an extra good thing added to something already good
example_en: A bonus on top of the promotion was the icing on the cake.

meaning_en: a small visible part of a much larger problem
example_en: These complaints are just the tip of the iceberg.

translation_ru: и в горе, и в радости
meaning_en: in good times and in bad
example_en: She stood by him through thick and thin.

meaning_en: to act without worrying about the risks
example_en: He threw caution to the wind and quit his job to travel.

meaning_en: to get married
example_en: They're tying the knot in June.

meaning_en: said as a joke, not meant seriously
example_en: His comment was tongue in cheek — don't take it personally.

meaning_en: to ignore something you know is wrong
example_en: Managers turned a blind eye to the late arrivals.

meaning_en: to persuade someone to do something they don't want to do
example_en: I had to twist his arm, but he agreed to come.

meaning_en: very close to you but not noticed
example_en: The keys were right under your nose the whole time.

meaning_en: uncertain, undecided
example_en: Our holiday plans are still up in the air.

meaning_en: to be very careful about what you say or do
example_en: Since the argument I've been walking on eggshells around her.

meaning_en: something in the past that is no longer important
example_en: We had our differences, but that's water under the bridge now.

meaning_en: something that will never happen
example_en: He'll apologise when pigs fly.

meaning_en: everything possible; the full extent
example_en: For the wedding she went the whole nine yards — orchestra, fireworks, the lot.

meaning_en: a useless and time-wasting search
example_en: He sent us on a wild goose chase across town.

meaning_en: with great success
example_en: She passed her driving test with flying colours.

meaning_en: spoken communication, especially recommendation
example_en: The restaurant became popular by word of mouth.

meaning_en: I completely agree
example_en: It's freezing today! — You can say that again.

idiom: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush
translation_ru: лучше синица в руках, чем журавль в небе
meaning_en: it is better to have something certain than the possibility of more
example_en: Take the offer — a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

idiom: A penny saved is a penny earned
meaning_en: money you save is just as valuable as money you earn
example_en: I bring lunch from home — a penny saved is a penny earned.

meaning_en: a hidden advantage
example_en: She's quiet now, but she has an ace up her sleeve.

meaning_en: threatening but not actually willing to act
example_en: Don't worry about him — he's all bark and no bite.

meaning_en: all day and all night
example_en: The bakery operates around the clock.

meaning_en: at a point where an important decision must be made
example_en: I'm at a crossroads in my career.

meaning_en: in strong disagreement
example_en: The two departments have been at loggerheads for months.

meaning_en: returning to the simplest, most important elements
example_en: Our coach took us back to basics this week.

meaning_en: to finish before the deadline
example_en: We just beat the clock with the submission.

meaning_en: to stop yourself from saying something
example_en: I had to bite my tongue when she criticised my work.

meaning_en: to earn money for the household
example_en: She's been bringing home the bacon since they moved.

idiom: Burn the candle at both ends
meaning_en: to exhaust yourself by doing too much
example_en: You'll get sick if you keep burning the candle at both ends.

meaning_en: surprised because not prepared
example_en: His question caught me off guard.

meaning_en: to discuss a problem to remove tension
example_en: Let's meet and clear the air about what happened.

meaning_en: no matter what difficulties arise
example_en: Come hell or high water, I'll finish this thesis.

meaning_en: to become more open and confident with others
example_en: Since starting drama club, she's come out of her shell.

meaning_en: very calm, especially under pressure
example_en: She stayed cool as a cucumber during the interview.

meaning_en: to hope for good luck
example_en: Cross your fingers that the weather holds.

meaning_en: feeling sad
example_en: He's been down in the dumps since the breakup.

meaning_en: to make someone very annoyed
example_en: That noise is driving me up the wall.

meaning_en: to admit that what you said was wrong
example_en: He had to eat his words after we proved him wrong.

translation_ru: и на нашей улице будет праздник
meaning_en: everyone gets a chance eventually
example_en: Don't give up — every dog has its day.

meaning_en: rare and infrequent
example_en: Sunny days here are few and far between.

meaning_en: to fight with great determination
example_en: She fought tooth and nail for that promotion.

meaning_en: to be exactly what is needed
example_en: This little café fits the bill perfectly.

meaning_en: to enjoy something a lot
example_en: He gets a real kick out of teaching kids to swim.

meaning_en: to start something happening
example_en: Let's get the ball rolling on the new project.

idiom: Give the benefit of the doubt
meaning_en: to believe someone is telling the truth even when uncertain
example_en: He's late, but I'll give him the benefit of the doubt this once.

meaning_en: to do the opposite of what is usual or expected
example_en: Quitting law school went against the grain in his family.

meaning_en: to be very kind and generous
example_en: She'll help anyone — she has a heart of gold.

meaning_en: to be very happy
example_en: She jumped for joy when she got the call.

meaning_en: to manage to survive financially or in difficulty
example_en: With two jobs I'm just keeping my head above water.

idiom: Like two peas in a pod
meaning_en: very similar, especially two people
example_en: The twins are like two peas in a pod.

meaning_en: to become very angry
example_en: Try not to lose your cool during the negotiation.
